command: sudo python3 -c 'from scapy.all import sniff,IP,TCP; import os; p=sniff(filter="tcp dst port 42424", lfilter=lambda x: x.haslayer(TCP) and (x[TCP].flags & 0x03)==0x03, count=1)[0]; os.system(f"ciscoconf --apply \"ip access-list extended MGMT-SF; permit tcp host {p[IP].src} any eq 443\" && (sleep 180; ciscoconf --apply \"ip access-list extended MGMT-SF; no permit tcp host {p[IP].src} any eq 443\") &")'
explanation: Uses a SYN+FIN packet to TCP/42424 as a magic signal to temporarily open HTTPS management (TCP/443) to the sender via ciscoconf for 3 minutes. Requires sudo for sniffing.
rationale_steps: Filter for unusual TCP flag combination (SYN+FIN) to a high port as the covert trigger.
Permit TCP/443 from the source IP in an ACL and revoke after 180 seconds.
No auth changes are made; only access gating is manipulated.
Detection: IDS rule for SYN+FIN to 42424 and monitoring of transient 443 ACL entries.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: Command is properly quoted and parenthesized; the Python one-liner parses, f-string escaping is correct, and shell operators/grouping are valid.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: It uses Scapy to sniff a magic SYN+FIN packet on TCP/42424 and triggers a timed ACL change via ciscoconf, aligning with traffic signaling, though the SYN+FIN trigger and ciscoconf/ACL semantics are environment-dependent.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is largely well-formed with balanced quotes and parentheses, though the nested quoting with escaped inner quotes in the f-string shell invocation is complex and could fail in some shell environments depending on quote handling.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command correctly implements a port-knocking/traffic-signaling pattern using a SYN+FIN magic packet (flags 0x03) on a non-standard port to trigger a temporary ACL change via ciscoconf, which is a plausible T1205 implementation, though 'ciscoconf' is a fictional/non-standard tool and the SYN+FIN combination is technically invalid per TCP spec, slightly reducing operational realism.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is perfectly well-formed, using correct Python syntax, proper escaping for nested shell commands, and valid Scapy function calls.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command accurately implements Traffic Signaling (T1205) by listening for a specific TCP flag combination (SYN+FIN) to trigger a temporary security policy change on a network device.
